- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

fault_reasoning.pod-cpu-hog: Deterministic temporal assignment: this event's startTime falls inside this fault's [injection_timestamp, injection_end_timestamp] window and no other fault was in flight.

name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}], "tools": [{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "configuration_view", "description": "Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"minified": {"type": "boolean", "description": "Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. If set to false, all contexts, clusters, auth-infos, and users are returned in the configuration. (Optional, default true)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "description": "List Kubernetes events (warnings, errors, state changes) for debugging and troubleshooting in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Namespace to retrieve the events from. If not provided, will list events from all namespaces"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "namespaces_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes namespaces in the current cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_log", "description": "Get logs from a Kubernetes node (kubelet, kube-proxy, or other system logs). This accesses node logs through the Kubernetes API proxy to the kubelet", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the node to get logs from"}, "query": {"type": "string", "description": "query specifies services(s) or files from which to return logs (required). Example: "kubelet" to fetch kubelet logs, "/<log-file-name>" to fetch a specific log file from the node (e.g., "/var/log/kubelet.log" or "/var/log/kube-proxy.log")"}, "tailLines": {"type": "integer", "description": "Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, 0 means all logs)", "default": 100, "minimum": 0}}, "required": ["name", "query"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_stats_summary", "description": "Get detailed resource usage statistics from a Kubernetes node via the kubelet's Summary API. Provides comprehensive metrics including CPU, memory, filesystem, and network usage at the node, pod, and container levels. On systems with cgroup v2 and kernel 4.20+, also includes PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics that show resource pressure for CPU, memory, and I/O. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/instrumentation/understand-psi-metrics/ for details on PSI metrics", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the node to get stats from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_top", "description": "List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Nodes or all nodes in the cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"label_selector": {"type": "string", "description": "Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'node-role.kubernetes.io/worker=') to filter nodes by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)", "pattern": "^([/_.\-A-Za-z0-9=, ()!])+$"}, "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Node to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Nodes if not provided)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_delete", "description": "Delete a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod to delete"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace to delete the Pod from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_exec", "description": "Execute a command in a Kubernetes Pod (shell access, run commands in container) in the current or provided namespace with the provided name and command", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"command": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}, "description": "Command to execute in the Pod container. The first item is the command to be run, and the rest are the arguments to that command. Example: ["ls", "-l", "/tmp"]"}, "container": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod container where the command will be executed (Optional)"}, "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod where the command will be executed"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace of the Pod where the command will be executed"}}, "required": ["name", "command"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_get", "description": "Get a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace to get the Pod from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes pods in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"fieldSelector": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}, {"role": "assistant", "content": null, "tool_calls": [{"id": "call_ldVkzU2OD4sg4iRHZHN7pupU", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list_in_namespace", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_9OVna3YGwKaSTXvhC3IqwHkQ", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_I7DMcgDLoweGqTdwY5GNMrfd", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_top", "arguments": "{"all_namespaces": false, "namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_7cd3dIcXjX32LVtEb3ArBax1", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "list_metrics", "arguments": "{"limit": 1000, "filter_pattern": "chaos|fault|inject|disrupt", "refresh_cache": false}"}}]}, {"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": "call_ldVkzU2OD4sg4iRHZHN7pupU", "content": "NAMESPACE   APIVERSION   KIND   NAME                                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS     AGE     IP             NODE                                NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES   LABELS

{"content": "{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "degraded",
    "status_justification": [
      "All 16 pods in namespace sock-shop are in Running phase and Ready, with no CrashLoopBackOff or Pending pods.",
      "Kubernetes events show only normal scheduling/startup activity except for transient startup probe failures on carts, which have since cleared.",
      "The flash-agent pod (2/2 containers, 1 recent restart) and its events indicate an injected disturbance/chaos-style agent operating in the namespace.",
      "Resource metrics via metrics.k8s.io are forbidden for the MCP service account, so live CPU/memory pressure cannot be confirmed; health assessment is based on status/events only."
    ],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "partial",
      "gaps": [
        "metrics_unavailable",
        "events_truncated",
        "no_metric_catalogue_tool"
      ],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score is slightly capped due to missing resource metrics and truncated events, but pod and event data are strong enough for a reasonably confident assessment."
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [
      "Namespace sock-shop currently has 16 pods, all in Running status and reporting Ready.",
      "Core sock-shop services (carts, catalogue, orders, payment, queue-master, rabbitmq, shipping, user, and their DBs) are all present and healthy.",
      "A flash-agent pod (app=flash-agent) is running with 2 containers and has 1 recent restart, which is atypical compared to the rest of the fleet.",
      "Events include transient Unhealthy startup probe failures for carts, but no ongoing readiness or liveness probe failures.",
      "Events for flash-agent show normal image pull and container creation; the name and labels strongly suggest a chaos/agent framework.",
      "The metrics-server (pods.metrics.k8s.io) is not accessible to the MCP service account in this namespace, preventing pod-level CPU/memory inspection.",
      "A Prometheus metric catalogue tool is not available (list_metrics tool is unknown), so metric-backed chaos detection via PromQL discovery is not possible."
    ],
    "analysis_approach": [

Resource metrics are not accessible to this analysis agent, slightly limiting confidence in performance-related conclusions.",

fault_reasoning.pod-cpu-hog: Deterministic temporal assignment: this event's startTime falls inside this fault's [injection_timestamp, injection_end_timestamp] window and no other fault was in flight.

name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}], "tools": [{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "configuration_view", "description": "Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"minified": {"type": "boolean", "description": "Return a minified version of the configuration. name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}, {"role": "assistant", "content": null, "tool_calls": [{"id": "call_jyLkltec0wucG9KN82WEGRbD", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list_in_namespace", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_fJ0n48B6vjNBpzmOW8utleEz", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_TLYZibM3NCVgQVCcMvMZ0GBj", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_top", "arguments": "{"all_namespaces": false, "namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_8hgyzSGzb6F031sfoVSC5Uvm", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "list_metrics", "arguments": "{"limit": 200, "filter_pattern": "chaos|fault|inject|disrupt", "refresh_cache": false}"}}]}, {"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": "call_jyLkltec0wucG9KN82WEGRbD", "content": "NAMESPACE   APIVERSION   KIND   NAME                                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS      AGE     IP             NODE                                NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES   LABELS

name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}], "tools": [{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "configuration_view", "description": "Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"minified": {"type": "boolean", "description": "Return a minified version of the configuration. fault_reasoning.pod-cpu-hog: Deterministic temporal assignment: this event's startTime falls inside this fault's [injection_timestamp, injection_end_timestamp] window and no other fault was in flight.

name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,

{"fault_name": "pod-network-loss", "ground_truth": {"fault_description_goal_remediation": {"goal": "Inject network packet loss on target pods to validate application resilience to degraded network conditions, retry logic, circuit breakers, and failover mechanisms.
", "remediation": "Identify pods experiencing packet loss. Inspect pod network configuration and container states. Delete affected pods to restore clean network state. Verify deployment health and confirm network connectivity resumes after chaos ends.
", "symptoms": ["High packet loss or dropped connections to target pods", "HTTP 5xx errors and request timeouts from dependent services", "Increased latency and retransmissions on pod network interface", "Warning events on target pods indicating network degradation", "Service mesh or load balancer health checks failing for affected pods", "Application logs showing connection timeouts and socket errors"]}, "ideal_course_of_action": [{"action": "List cluster events", "detail": "List Kubernetes events across all namespaces to detect network-related warnings, pod failures, or unhealthy endpoints triggered by the packet loss injection.
", "step": 1}, {"action": "List pods in affected namespace", "detail": "List all pods in the target namespace to identify pods in degraded, Error, or CrashLoopBackOff state due to network loss.
", "step": 2}, {"action": "Get affected pod details", "detail": "Retrieve the full spec and status of the affected pod to inspect container states, restart counts, and network configuration.
", "step": 3}, {"action": "Get pod logs", "detail": "Retrieve logs from the affected pod to identify network-related errors such as connection timeouts, socket resets, and I/O errors.
", "step": 4}, {"action": "Exec into pod to check network connectivity", "detail": "Execute network diagnostic commands (ping, curl) inside the pod to confirm packet loss on the network interface.
", "step": 5}, {"action": "Check pod resource consumption", "detail": "Check CPU and memory usage of pods in the target namespace to correlate network issues with resource pressure.
", "step": 6}, {"action": "Check node-level network stats", "detail": "Get node stats summary to inspect network usage at the node level and identify abnormal transmit/receive error counts.
", "step": 7}, {"action": "Query Prometheus for network error metrics", "detail": "Execute a PromQL query for network-related metrics (e.g., container_network_transmit_errors_total) to quantify packet loss during the chaos window.
", "step": 8}, {"action": "Query Prometheus for HTTP error rate", "detail": "Execute a PromQL query for HTTP 5xx error rates to measure application-level impact of the network packet loss.
", "step": 9}, {"action": "Delete affected pods", "detail": "Delete pods stuck in error or degraded state so the controller recreates them with clean network configuration after chaos ends.
", "step": 10}, {"action": "Verify deployment health", "detail": "Get the deployment resource to confirm desired replicas are running and the rollout is healthy after remediation.
", "step": 11}, {"action": "Scale deployment if needed", "detail": "Scale the deployment to ensure sufficient healthy replicas are available to handle traffic after network recovery.
", "step": 12}, {"action": "Validate network connectivity restored", "detail": "Exec into a pod and run connectivity checks (ping, curl) to confirm network packet loss has ceased.
", "step": 13}, {"action": "Confirm application recovery via Prometheus", "detail": "Query Prometheus for HTTP error rates and latency to verify the application has fully recovered.
", "step": 14}], "ideal_tool_usage_trajectory": [{"command": "Events: List", "purpose": "Detect network-related warnings, pod failures, and unhealthy endpoint events", "step": 1, "tool": "Events: configuration of the affected pod", "step": 3, "tool": "Pods: Get", "tool_available": true}, {"command": "Pods: Log name=<affected-pod-name> namespace=<target-namespace>", "purpose": "Retrieve application logs to find connection timeouts, socket errors, and I/O failures", "step": 4, "tool": "Pods: Log", "tool_available": true}, {"command": "Pods: Exec name=<affected-pod-name> namespace=<target-namespace> command='ping -c 4 <target-service> && curl -s -o /dev/null -w %{http_code} <target-service-url>'", "purpose": "Verify packet loss and connectivity degradation from inside the affected pod", "step": 5, "tool": "Pods: Exec", "tool_available": true}, {"command": "Pods: Top namespace=<target-namespace>", "purpose": "Check resource consumption of affected pods to rule out resource pressure", "step": 6, "tool": "Pods: Top", "tool_available": true}, {"command": "Node: Stats Summary name=<node-name>", "purpose": "Inspect node-level network stats to identify abnormal transmit/receive error counts", "step": 7, "tool": "Node: Stats Summary", "tool_available": true}, {"command": "Execute PromQL Query query=sum(rate(container_network_transmit_errors_total{namespace='<target-namespace>'}[5m])) by (pod)", "purpose": "Quantify network transmit errors during chaos injection window", "step": 8, "tool": name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.
